course_description:
This course prepares pre-service teachers to teach classroom music in the secondary school. Pre-service teachers will be provided with opportunity to develop knowledge and understanding of relevant curriculum documents. The course will engage pre-service teachers in analysis of recent disciplinary developments in scholarship and professional practice in the teaching of classroom music. They will be provided with opportunities to develop the appropriate knowledge and teaching strategies to plan for effective learning in classroom music. Pre-service teachers will demonstrate the ability to (i) design, implement and evaluate learning and teaching sequences and discipline specific assessment tasks; and (ii) identify and design discipline specific opportunities for professional engagement and communication within the school context, teaching networks and the broader community.

This course has an approved course date variation from the standard semester schedule. Please visit the Course Date Variations page for further details.

This course aims to foster skilled and informed practitioners who exhibit positive attitudes towards teaching and who are able to impart a love of music and learning to students in the Secondary school. As part of this course, participants reflect upon, critically discuss, develop and refine an informed working knowledge of the issues relevant to music education in particular. This course involves participants in the principles and processes of music education, developingﾠteaching skills necessary for the successful implementation of music education programs in schools.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate understanding of relevant content and critical analysis of recent disciplinary developments in scholarship and professional practice in the teaching of Music. APST 2.1

Demonstrate ability to apply knowledge and understanding of research into how students learn and knowledge of relevant curriculum documents to the development of appropriate discipline based learning goals and teaching strategies for students of varying abilities. APST 1.2, 1.5, 2.1, 3.1

Demonstrate the application of knowledge and skills to practice through the design, implementation and evaluation of effective learning and teaching sequences and discipline specific assessment tasks that draw on relevant curriculum documents, range of effective teaching resources, a full range of assessment and reporting strategies, and ICT knowledge that enables teaching strategies that expands learning. APST 2.2, 2.3, 2.6, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 5.1
Demonstrate comprehensive and in-depth knowledge and understanding of literacy and numeracy teaching strategies and their application in Music. APST 2.5

Demonstrate application of knowledge and skills to practice through responding to feedback, and identifying and designing discipline specific opportunities for professional learning engagement and communication within the school context, teaching networks and the broader community and understand the rationale for this professional practice in relation to improved student learning. APST 6.3, 6.4, 7.4
Demonstrate clear, fluent and coherent communication skills in English consistent with personal, professional and academic convention. APST 2.1

assessment_details.title: Junior Unit Plan
assessment_details.task_description: You are required to develop a unit plan suitable for Years 7-10 classroom music as part of the Australian Curriculum. The document should represent an overview of 9 weeks of planning (for a school where students get 4x30-minute lessons per week), incorporating 2-3 hours of detailed planning (lesson plans) and repertoire to be studied. Ensure that your teaching strategies are varied, integrate making and responding and incorporate some music technology. Please note that while you may use topics taught during your own Music Education experience, and even develop and adapt a unit of work from ACARA or QCAA, the work included for this assessment needs to be your own. All Unit Plans should include the following: Unit title Introductory information Include Intended year level. Context Statement: Briefly describe the school context applicable to the learning experiences described in your learning sequence. Anticipated prior music knowledge, understanding and skills needed to ensure student success. Rationale (drawing on relevant academic literature and the curriculum) Detail the purpose and value of this unit of work, and justification of the approach chosen. Learning Objectives Include specific learning objectives - linked to Curriculum. Repertoire You are required to collect five (5) pieces of music appropriate for use in a classroom context for this unit. It is intended that you use this opportunity to extend your personal repertoire and source interesting works that are new to you, which will be useful in your own classroom practice. For each piece of repertoire, you should: Justify your selection of the repertoire, giving a brief explanation of its relevance to the development of the student musician (approx. 100 words). Identify how it relates to the knowledge and skills for the relevant year level (referring specifically to the Australian Curriculum) Provide at least one learning activity to be associated with the repertoire, worded as it would be for students. A worksheet/activity is a good example of this. Ensure that across the repertoire selected, you provide examples of activities in making and responding to music. Assessment tasks Two assessment tasks that enable the demonstration of relevant content descriptors. Attach the relevant standards matrix to each. Outline of intended learning Provide the overview of the learning (9 weeks) A unit overview in table form using dot points. Provide sufficient detail so that lesson objectives, lesson activities, and links to curriculum are all clear to a reader. Provide evidence of varied and authentic learning experiences Include due dates for draft and final submission of two assessment tasks Suggested resources/repertoire (provide copies/links to these in appendices) Detailed lesson plans (4 X 30-minute lessons) Ensure the style of learning/teaching and the focus of the lesson is varied across these lessons. They do not have to be sequential. You may use a lesson planning template that you are familiar with, or the ones provided in class. Appendices Include resources, original worksheets and scores/link to recordings of repertoire used, and/or suitable extensions. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit the assessment electronically via the assignment link on the course website. By submitting your assignment, you are certifying that is it your original work and that it has not been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at university.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Senior Unit of Work
assessment_details.task_description: Using the Unit 1 Designs (see Section 2, General Music Syllabus), create a coherent and exciting 10-week unit of work suitable for teaching from either Term 1 or 2, in Year 11. The unit needs to demonstrate alignment with the pedagogical and conceptual frameworks in the syllabus. It must include and feature varied and authentic learning strategies, innovative resources and some music technology. The o utline of intended learning should be provided in table form. Include a composition or performance task that aligns with the General Music Syllabus. Active and focused teaching of composition and/or performance concepts and skills should be evident in the plan. Provide three integrated and detailed lesson plans. Here is the suggested outline: Rationale. Justification for a chosen focus (include references) The school context, which may impact upon the unit design, resources and other considerations (this can include references) Detailed identification of the subject matter to be taught (refer specifically to syllabus, as well as other references) An overview of how your unit incorporates imaginative and innovative learning design to engage students (include references) Detailed identification of strategies to differentiate teaching to meet the specific learning needs of students across the full range of abilities (refer specifically to syllabus, as well as other references) An overview of the relationship between the selected assessment tools and the appropriate and authentic provision of learning in music education (refer specifically to syllabus, as well as other references) Unit Objectives Include the relevant unit objectives for this 10-week plan. Resources List and link to core resources (for example 3-5 pieces or extracts from larger works, online interaction spaces, in-person scheduled concerts). Outline of intended learning - a sequence of contextualized learning experiences. Table form using dot points, but with sufficient detail to demonstrate the following: evidence of strategies which align with pedagogical and conceptual frameworks highlighted in the syllabus evidence of imaginative and varied learning experiences evidence of authentic learning experiences alignment to syllabus objectives and subject matter. Include due dates for draft and final submission of one assessment task Detailed lesson plans Three lesson plans all demonstrating an integrated learning design. Assessment tasks One assessment task, either composition or performance that enables the demonstration of the assessment objectives. Relevant syllabus conditions aligned to the task. APSTS: 1.2; 1.5; 2.1; 2.2; 2.3; 2.5; 2.6; 3.1; 3.2; 3.3; 3.4; 5.1 Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Professional development for colleagues and online learning resource
assessment_details.due_date: 20/10/2026 Presentations will occur during class. The written submission is to be uploaded to TurnItIn (and Blackboard discussion groups) prior to presenting.
assessment_details.task_description: As a new graduate, you are well placed to engage colleague teachers with new approaches/ideas in music education, sharing innovative ways that your chosen issue can be applied to practice in the school environment. In consultation with your lecturer and in response to feedback you have received from supervising teachers, identify an issue or question of relevance to you in your future practice as a teacher of music. Your topic should be linked to your own identified professional learning needs and should make a clear contribution to improving the impact you can make to student learning. For this assessment, you are to engage your peers in an investigation of your chosen issue through a ‘professional development’ seminar. Assist them to come to their own conclusions through participation in a thought-provoking workshop presented by you in a small discussion group (15-20 minutes). You may choose to frame your PD as a demonstration of ‘how to’ address a difficult issue specific to music education. Some examples of ‘issues’ include (but are not limited to): “How to incorporate Hip Hop Pedagogy in the Senior Classroom” or “How to integrate composition, performance and analysis in ways that enhance student motivation” or “how to design authentic, inquiry-driven performance assessments” or “how to assess compositions created in conjunction with AI”. It is strongly recommended that you check your topic or issue with your lecturer prior to the seminar date. Your workshop should be highly interactive, engaging your peers with your chosen topic, and should keep strictly to the time limit. You must incorporate an online resource created by you for your peers to ‘take home’ or refer to. The online learning resource should be able to be utilised by school students, in the style of a YouTube lesson, demonstrating your understanding of the issue. The online learning resource should also be able to be used by other teachers in their classrooms. Submission is to be through the discussion board on Blackboard, AND Turnitin and must include: Link to the online resource demonstrating application of theory to practice. One double-sided page of notes (referenced) summarising the key ideas in your PD, for use by your colleagues. Dot points or diagrams demonstrating synthesis of the ideas and their practical application are encouraged. The notes must demonstrate how the PD critically draws on a minimum of 10 recent (last 10 years) academic journal articles or book chapters, in addition to contemporary syllabus documents. Additional resources can be provided to colleagues, but are not required. Students are required to present their workshop live in class, and also video record their presentation, and upload it to Blackboard for a record of the presentation. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit page of notes (which includes active link to your YouTube resource) on Discussion board prior to presentation, so that your peers can access.
Also submit page of notes (which includes active link to your YouTube resource) via TurnItIn link on Blackboard.
Submit video of your presentation via link on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Weekly reflection task and summary reflection
assessment_details.task_description: Engagement with the learning in this course will provide students with the knowledge and skills to succeed as Secondary Classroom Music Teachers. Central to this is developing skills to reflect reflexively on their learning, and to seek and solve practical and professional problems as they arise over the course of the year. To further these skills, students will reflect on their questions and learnings after each class, referencing required readings, workshop activities, field trips and practicum experiences as they go. Each documented response will be marked as either a pass or fail, and will attract ½ percent weighting for the course. The final 400 word written reflection on learning summarises the student’s most significant learning over the year, detailing what has been learnt in relation to the learning outcomes of the course, referring to disciplinary developments in scholarship and professional practice, how students learn and the implications of this for planning for learning in music, intersections of theoretical notions of excellence in music education and curriculum requirements, how to design assessment, provide and respond to feedback. Conclude with a statement of intent regarding steps you will take towards becoming the teacher you wish to be, in your future practice (400 words). This final reflection attracts 4 percent weighting for the course (20 percent total weighting for this task). The overall grade for this task is calculated as a percentage by taking the points scored across all eighteen weekly responses (½ point per week x 18 weeks, as well as the final reflection (2 points), and comparing it to the total points possible e.g., 16 points allocated for responses/reflections out of 20 total points: 16/20 x 100 = 80% This percentage is mapped to the UQ grading schema (below) to display a grade: 84.5% and 100% = 7 74.5% and less than 84.5% = 6 64.5% and less than 74.5% = 5 49.5% and less than 64.5% = 4 46.5% and less than 49.5% = 3 23.5% and less than 46.5% = 2 0 and less than 23.5% = 1 Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission via blackboard every week (due on the Friday of the relevant week, by 1pm), plus written reflection due in the final teaching week of the year-long course, on the Friday, by 1pm.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Please note: Students are NOT able to apply for an extension via the Unitask portal. Please contact the Course Coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available if there is a genuine reason why you cannot attend the class.
Late submission
Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.

learning_activities.topic: Workshop 1 Music learning, musicianship and lesson planning. Developing a professional identity, creativity. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Workshop 2 Introducing the Australian Curriculum: Music 7-10 v9. Musicianship (Workshop): Bring along your prepared work for this workshop. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Workshop 3 Unit design and planning. Secondary school curriculum: Junior Music Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Workshop 4 Secondary school curriculum: Senior Music. Identities lecture for University of Toronto and University of Queensland Students *(same time, online) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Workshop 5 Designing and assessing performance. Designing musicology learning. Focussing on pedagogy and curriculum relating to musicology across the High School (Junior secondary and Senior Curriculum) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Workshop 6 Unit planning in the Junior School. Excursion to The Gap State High (Field Work): Students are to make their own way there and back. Be sure to take Blue Cards and observation sheets. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Mid-Semester Break Learning outcomes: L06

Workshop 7 Designing, marking composition assessment. Focussing on composition across the High School (Junior and Senior Curriculum). Composing Careers – a songwriting strategy linking identity development with creativity. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Week 12 (20 Apr - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Break for Professional Experience No classes scheduled while undertaking professional experience (placement) in an accompanying course. Learning outcomes: L05, L06
Workshop 8 Tech in Music Education. Lego as a metaphor for composition. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06

Workshop 9 - Special Learning Online Unit planning, lesson sequencing, and explicit teaching and scaffolding. Lecture 8 is split into five mini-lectures, with each followed by tutor-led activities. Note: Detailed information about the content of Lecture 8 is provided as follows: Mini lectures during Workshop 8 In mini-lecture 1 , preservice teachers learn about beginning instruction effectively, the importance of scaffolding, and how independent problem solving imposes high cognitive load for learners new to content or skills. (CC 2.2.2, 2.2.6 – taught). Mini-lecture 2 teaches about brain structure, function, and development, and how memory is processed, stored, and retrieved (CC 2.2.5 – taught). Mini-lecture 3 teaches key principles of Cognitive Load Theory, Retrieval Practice, and Spaced Practice (CC 2.1.2 – taught). Mini-lecture 4 teaches the value and development of worked examples (CC 1.3.3, 2.2.4 – taught), and the need for explicit teaching of subject-specific knowledge and skills that become increasingly complex and independent (CC 2.1.1 – taught). Mini-lecture 5 teaches key features of coherent and deliberate planning (2.1.1 – taught), and how to sequence tasks in lessons (CC 2.1.3 – taught). Practical tasks following mini lectures As a tutor-led task following mini-lecture 1 , preservice teachers develop learning objectives for lesson sequences and task introductions with well-defined goals (CC 2.2.2 – practised). After mini-lecture 2 , preservice teachers engage in a recall task (CC 2.2.5 – practised). After mini-lecture 3 , preservice teachers incorporate spacing and retrieval practices into a lesson or sequence (CC 2.1.2 – practised). After mini-lecture 4 , preservice teachers observe tutor modelling of subject-specific worked examples, and design short tasks for students who are unfamiliar with a curriculum concept (CC 1.3.3, 2.2.4 – practiced). After mini-lecture 5 , preservice teachers revisit a task from mini-lecture 1, and design and discuss activities that incorporate spacing and/or retrieval practice (CC 2.1.1, 2.1.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L01

Workshop 10 Planning for Identities Senior Unit: Developing task for Senior Music: Identities Selecting and using repertoire/resources: Revise assessment, reflect on practicum, collaborative junior pedagogy and curriculum strategies. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06
Workshop 11 Senior Unit Planning 1: Designing digital based learning Unit Planning 2: Bring your Senior Unit drafts to class. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Workshop 12 External assessment, endorsement. Improvisation in the classroom. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 16 To Week 21 (10 Aug - 20 Sep)
learning_activities.topic: Break for Professional Experience No classes scheduled while undertaking professional experience (placement) in an accompanying course Learning outcomes: L04, L06

Workshop 13 Music Extension. Contemporary trends in music education around the world: issues to consider in linking research/philosophies/pedagogies/curriculum design for music education into the future. Getting ready for Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Mid-Semester Break Learning outcomes: L03, L06
Workshop 14 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander musics. Music Education as Leisure. Learning outcomes: L01
Workshop 15 Music Technology. Context-based problem solving. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Workshop 16 Student-led discussion of big issues (over two meetings) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Workshop 17 Scenario-based workshop. Summarising learning and launching into the career: Resource sharing, drop-in sessions and SECaTs. Learning outcomes: L01, L05
